title: Why I started this blog
content: I keep solving the same problems twice because I never write anything down. This blog is my fix. Plain-language notes on Go, my homelab, and the system design stuff I'm slowly figuring out as a junior dev.
excerpt: A place to write things down so I stop solving them twice.

title: My first month as a junior backend engineer
content: I joined Ridgeline in August and spent the first month mostly reading code and asking questions. Here's what actually helped: pairing early, keeping a daily log, and not pretending to understand things I didn't.
excerpt: What the first month on a real backend team felt like.

title: Table-driven tests are my favorite Go pattern
content: Once I understood table-driven tests in Go, I stopped dreading writing tests. One slice of cases, one loop, easy to add edges. Here's the template I copy into every package.
excerpt: The Go testing pattern that made me stop dreading tests.

title: Setting up my homelab Kubernetes cluster
content: I run a small k3s cluster at home across a mini PC and two Raspberry Pis. This post covers the hardware, why I picked k3s over full kubeadm, and the networking that took me a weekend to untangle.
excerpt: Hardware, k3s, and the networking that ate a weekend.

title: Why I self-host
content: It's not about saving money, because it doesn't. I self-host because I learn more from running things than reading about them. Every outage at home is a lesson I can carry into work.
excerpt: Not cheaper, but the best lab I could ask for.

title: Building Ridgeline in Go
content: Ridgeline is my self-hosted read-it-later tool. Go for the backend, Postgres for storage, React on the front. This post walks through the project layout and why I kept the architecture boring on purpose.
excerpt: The architecture behind my main side project.

title: Postgres indexing lessons
content: I spent an evening watching a Ridgeline query crawl, then learned more about Postgres indexes than any blog post taught me. EXPLAIN ANALYZE, partial indexes, and why my composite index column order was backwards.
excerpt: What a slow query taught me about Postgres indexes.

title: Understanding context in Go
content: context.Context confused me for months. It clicked when I stopped thinking of it as magic and started treating it as a cancellation signal that flows down the call stack. Cancellation, deadlines, and what not to put in it.
excerpt: Context finally clicked when I stopped overthinking it.

title: Reverse proxying my homelab with Caddy
content: I moved from nginx to Caddy at home mostly for automatic HTTPS. One Caddyfile fronts Ridgeline, Glanceboard, and a couple of internal dashboards. Config and the DNS setup that makes it work behind CGNAT.
excerpt: One Caddyfile to front the whole homelab.

title: Goroutines and channels without footguns
content: I leaked goroutines for weeks before I understood the patterns. Always know who closes the channel, always have a way to stop, and lean on errgroup. The concurrency rules I now follow every time.
excerpt: The concurrency rules I follow after leaking goroutines.

title: Glanceboard: a homelab status dashboard
content: My second side project, Glanceboard, watches my homelab services and shows uptime at a glance. Smaller than Ridgeline on purpose. Here's the design and how it pings each service without hammering them.
excerpt: A small uptime dashboard for my home cluster.

title: Dockerfiles I'm not embarrassed by
content: My early Dockerfiles were 800MB and slow to build. Multi-stage builds, a scratch or distroless final image, and proper layer caching got my Go images under 20MB. Before-and-after walkthrough.
excerpt: Multi-stage builds got my Go images under 20MB.

title: Junior dev on-call survival guide
content: My first on-call rotation was terrifying. What actually helped: reading the runbooks before the week starts, knowing how to roll back, and that it's fine to wake up a senior at 3am. You are not expected to know everything.
excerpt: What got me through my first on-call rotation.

title: Designing the Ridgeline sync API
content: Letting the Ridgeline browser extension sync bookmarks offline meant thinking about conflict resolution for the first time. Last-write-wins was tempting but wrong. How I landed on a simple version-vector approach.
excerpt: Conflict resolution for an offline-first sync API.

title: Testing CI for solo side projects
content: Guest post from Priya. You don't need a 12-stage pipeline for a side project. A single GitHub Actions workflow that runs go test and a linter catches most of what breaks. Keep it fast or you'll start skipping it.
excerpt: A guest take on keeping CI light for side projects.

title: Adding Stripe payments to Ridgeline
content: Ridgeline makes a little money now. Wiring up Stripe Checkout and webhooks was less code than I feared, but handling webhook idempotency and failed payments was the real work. Notes for the next solo dev doing this.
excerpt: Wiring up Stripe Checkout and webhooks the careful way.

title: Terraform for a one-person infrastructure
content: I use Terraform for the small bit of AWS that Ridgeline touches: an S3 bucket, a Lambda, some IAM. Overkill for the size, but it means I can rebuild it all from scratch. State management when you're a team of one.
excerpt: Terraform when your whole team is just you.

title: A frontend dev's notes on the Ridgeline UI
content: Guest post from Leo. Jason's backend is solid but the UI needed love. I rebuilt the reading view in React with Tailwind, focused on typography and a fast keyboard-driven workflow. What I changed and why.
excerpt: Leo's pass over the Ridgeline reading experience.

title: Profiling a slow Go endpoint with pprof
content: One Ridgeline endpoint was burning CPU for no obvious reason. pprof pointed straight at a JSON marshal in a hot loop. The fix was caching the encoded response. How to read a flame graph without getting lost.
excerpt: pprof found the hot loop I never would have guessed.

title: What a year of self-hosting taught me
content: A year into running my homelab, the biggest lesson is boring: backups you've actually tested, and alerts you actually read. The fancy Kubernetes stuff matters less than the basics. A reflective wrap-up.
excerpt: One year in, the boring lessons mattered most.

title: Draft: migrating Ridgeline off a single Postgres node
content: Early notes on adding a read replica before I actually need one. Mostly thinking out loud about connection pooling and read/write splitting. Not ready to publish, the numbers don't justify it yet.
excerpt: Work in progress on scaling the Ridgeline database.

title: Draft: design handoff for solo developers
content: Guest draft from Jake. How a designer and a solo dev can work in Figma without a heavyweight process. Rough notes from helping with Ridgeline and Glanceboard mockups. Needs more examples before it ships.
excerpt: A designer's rough notes on working with solo devs.
